text: Hello, this is Dr. Intavi. Today I will be discussing a laparoscopic repair of perforated peptic ulcer. This patient presented with sepsis and free air on the CT scan. I performed it laparoscopically with three small incisions, two five millimeter ports and one ten millimeter port.

Upon entering the abdominal cavity, a significant amount of pus and bile is visible. The surgeon clears the area, revealing inflammation and erythema throughout the abdominal cavity, including the abdominal wall, gallbladder, and liver, along with fibrinous material indicating a recent perforation.

The surgeon removes the omentum covering the peptic ulcer, revealing a small ulcer approximately two millimeters in width. Using an indoor stitch technique, the surgeon begins suturing to close the ulcer primarily.
The surgeon places a full-thickness suture to close the small peptic ulcer, ensuring a secure grip. An omentum patch is then applied over the ulcer to reinforce the closure.

The surgeon demonstrates the closure of a perforated peptic ulcer, with visible sutures securing the ulcer site. A roll of omentum is placed over the closure to aid in approximation, and an additional piece of omentum is prepared to further buttress the repair.
